summary: Joel Spolsky shares statistics about Stack Overflow's growth and its relationship to Stack Overflow Careers. He notes that higher-reputation users are disproportionately likely to submit CVs, which validates the platform's pitch to employers. Stack Overflow has reached 6 million monthly unique visitors, making it arguably the largest programming site in the world. Despite this scale, the site struggles to attract major advertising deals because it refuses to run animated or aggressive ad formats, a trade-off Joel embraces as worth the lost revenue.
key_points: Higher Stack Overflow reputation correlates strongly with participation in Stack Overflow Careers, validating the platform's value proposition to employers
Stack Overflow reached the 740th ranked site globally with 6 million monthly uniques and steady growth
Stack Overflow appears to have more traffic than any other programming site, including MSDN
The site refuses animated ads on principle, costing significant advertising revenue from agencies running major campaigns like the Visual Studio 2010 launch
Advertising agencies prefer aggressive, animated ad formats and are uninterested in sites that only offer static placements
money_quotes: When you search for CVs on Stack Overflow, you are looking at some pretty gosh darn good programmers.

We don't accept any kind of animated ads on Stack Overflow, because, well, they're evil, so we lose a lot of revenue from advertising agencies who are looking for the most aggressive possible ways to get in people's faces.
Whatever. Don't care. We hate animated ads and I'm pretty sure our users do, too.
Agencies really don't like us. Now if we go to video units with fly-over… oh they'll start loving us!
Are there any sites out there for programmers with more traffic than Stack Overflow? I haven't found any.
key_insight: Stack Overflow's willingness to sacrifice advertising revenue by refusing animated ads reflects a user-first philosophy that, paradoxically, helped build the massive engaged audience that makes the platform valuable in the first place.
